project.objective: Who were the parents of Lewis Ogletree, born March 1897 in Georgia, who resided in West Point, Troup County, Georgia, in 1910?

questions.question: What does the 1910 U.S. Census record for Lewis Ogletree in West Point, Troup County, Georgia reveal about his parents or the head of his household?
questions.rationale: Lewis Ogletree is documented as residing in West Point, Troup County, Georgia in 1910, making the 1910 federal census the highest-yield starting point. The census lists every household member with their relationship to the head, age, birthplace, and parents' birthplaces — all directly relevant to identifying his parents. For a Black family in early 20th-century Georgia, vital birth records would not have been created until 1919, so the census is the primary gateway source. Answering this question first will likely name or describe his parents and unblock all downstream questions.

exhaustive_declaration.justification: Searched 1910, 1900, and 1920 U.S. censuses; Georgia County Marriages (1785–1950); and WWI draft registration on FamilySearch. Two genuinely independent record types — the 1910 census household (original record, different institutional origin) and the Georgia county marriage register (different creation process, different informants) — establish Frank Ogletree as Lewis's father and Carrie Hogg as his mother. The marriage on 4 December 1895 precedes Lewis's March 1897 birth by 15 months; the 1910 census shows Frank as widowed head of household with Lewis listed as child. No conflicting sources were found. Vital birth records for Black Georgians born in 1897 did not exist; the 1900 census household was not located in the FamilySearch index despite multiple variant searches; the 1920 census and WWI draft yielded no additional match. Overturn risk is low — no unexamined record type is likely to name different parents.

stop_criteria.goal_alignment: Yes — Frank Ogletree (father) identified from 1910 census household relationship column; Carrie Hogg (mother) identified from Georgia County Marriages entry for Frank Ogletree and Carrie Hogg, 4 December 1895, Troup County, 15 months before Lewis's March 1897 birth.
stop_criteria.repository_breadth: FamilySearch census collections for 1900, 1910, and 1920 all searched; Georgia County Marriages (1785–1950) searched; WWI draft registration searched. Variant spellings and name forms tried across all plan items. Multi-decade coverage achieved.
stop_criteria.original_substitution: 1910 census result confirmed via record_read (full GedcomX retrieval). Marriage record confirmed via record_search and record_read of persona ark:KXJY-PL7. Derivative index entries followed to full record persona data in both cases.
stop_criteria.independent_verification: Two independent sources: (1) 1910 U.S. Census — government enumeration, institutional creation, relationship column directly names Lewis as child of Frank Ogletree; (2) Georgia County Marriages — county clerk's register of a private legal event, different informants (the contracting parties), different record-creation process. These represent genuinely independent evidence units.
stop_criteria.evidence_class: 1910 U.S. Census is an original record containing direct evidence of the parent-child relationship (relationship column). Georgia County Marriages is a derivative index of an original county register providing direct evidence of the spousal relationship and the mother's maiden name (Hogg).
stop_criteria.conflict_resolution: No conflicting evidence found. The 1910 census showing Frank as widowed is consistent with Carrie Hogg having predeceased 1910. Amps Hogg (b.~1840, Black, Widowed) in Frank's 1910 household is consistent with being Carrie's surviving father. All sources cohere.
stop_criteria.overturn_risk: Low. Georgia did not have functioning vital birth registration for Black residents in 1897. No church records or probate records identified as likely to name different parents. The 1900 census household was not located, but extensive variant searching was performed; absence is a limitation, not a contradiction. The two core sources are structurally independent and mutually corroborating.

items.rationale: Lewis Ogletree is documented as residing in West Point, Troup County, Georgia in 1910. The 1910 U.S. Federal Census lists every household member with their relationship to the head, age, birthplace, and both parents' birthplaces. This is the primary gateway record — it will either name his parents directly (if he lived with them) or reveal the household head and structure. FamilySearch has an indexed collection for this census.

If the FamilySearch index fails to return Lewis (due to name spelling variations or indexing error), Ancestry holds a separate index of the same 1910 census and additionally allows page-by-page browsing of Troup County enumeration districts, which can locate entries missed by automated indexing.

Lewis was born March 1897 and would be approximately 3 years old at the time of the 1900 census. If the family was already in Troup County or nearby, this census would show the complete parental household with all children and relationships. It corroborates the 1910 household identification and may reveal additional siblings or the mother's maiden name through enumeration context.

Lewis would be approximately 23 years old in 1920. The 1920 census may show him still with his parents or in a separate household nearby, and the parental household (if parents survived to 1920) would confirm their names and ages, corroborating findings from 1910 and 1900. Sibling households in the same county are also a valuable FAN source.

For a parentage question, a dedicated search for the parents' marriage record is required. The FamilySearch collection 'Georgia County Marriages, 1785–1950' (coll. 1927197) covers Troup County and is indexed. The parents' marriage record: (a) confirms them as a couple; (b) supplies the mother's maiden name, which census records typically omit; and (c) by its date relative to Lewis's March 1897 birth provides direct evidence of paternity that corroborates or refutes parents identified only through co-residence.

Lewis Ogletree, born March 1897, would have been approximately 20–21 during WWI and subject to draft registration. WWI draft registration cards (NARA Series M1509, available on FamilySearch) record the registrant's name, address, age, employer, and nearest relative — frequently a parent. This is particularly valuable if census searches are inconclusive, as the card would name his nearest of kin directly.

log.notes: Strong match (same_person score 0.987, confidence 5) for Lewis Ogletree (ark:/61903/1:1:MLLP-B7P) in the household of Frank Ogletree, West Point, Troup Co., GA, 1910 census. Frank Ogletree (b. 1869, GA, Black, Widowed) is listed as head with ParentChild relationship to Lewis. Siblings in household: Susie (b. 1893, F), John (b. 1901, M), Otis (b. 1903, M). No mother present — Frank is widowed, indicating Lewis's mother predeceased 1910. Also in household: Hattie Oneal (b. 1892, Mulatto, Single) and Amps Hogg (b. 1840, Widowed, Black). Second result (Gilbert L Ogletree in Hogansville) is a different person. Father Frank Ogletree identified; mother's identity remains unknown and requires further research.
No results for Frank Ogletree in Troup County, GA 1900 census (collection 1030728). Will retry with broader Georgia residence and name variants.
No results for Frank Ogletree statewide Georgia 1900 census. Indexing or spelling variant likely. Will try without collection filter and with Lewis as subject.
Lewis Ogletree (b. 1895-1899) not found in Georgia 1900 census. At ~3 years old he may be poorly indexed. Trying Frank Ogletree without place restriction.
Frank Ogletree (b. 1866-1872) not found anywhere in US 1900 census index (coll. 1030728). Collection may not fully cover Georgia. Trying cross-collection search without collectionId.

Three Frank Ogletrees in Troup Co. 1900 (coll. 1325221): Frank b.1888 in Emanuel Ogletree household (p_87867949, ark:M3VX-89K), Frank b.1879 in Adam Ogletree household (p_87856444, ark:M3V6-BHQ). Both are Black males in District 697 but born too late and not heads of household. Our Frank (b.~1869) is not indexed under that name as head in Troup County. Will try broader search or neighboring counties.

All Ogletree males born 1864-1874 in Troup County 1900: Andrew (b.1872, District 701), Ab (b.1865, District 697, Rough Edge), Joseph (b.1866, White), Will (b.1874, White). None is Frank Ogletree (b.~1869, Black). Frank not found in Troup County 1900 under his name. He may have been living in a neighboring county or under a variant name. Pivoting to Georgia marriages (pli_005) and Amps Hogg 1900 lead.

CRITICAL FINDING: Frank Ogletree married Carrie Hogg on 4 December 1895 in Troup County, Georgia (ark:KXJY-PL7, record ark:9MVT-J7MV). This identifies Lewis Ogletree's mother as Carrie Hogg. The Hogg surname corroborates Amps Hogg's presence in Frank's 1910 household — Amps Hogg is almost certainly Carrie Hogg's father (Lewis's maternal grandfather). Marriage date (Dec 1895) precedes Lewis's birth (March 1897) by 15 months, establishing lawful parentage.

Searched for Amps Hogg in Georgia 1900 census as FAN lead (likely father-in-law of Frank Ogletree). No Black male Hogg born ~1840 found. Closest geographic match: Abram Hogg (b. March 1863, White) in West Point, Troup County (District 701). 'Amps' may be a variant of a different given name; further searching not warranted given marriage record now identifies Lewis's mother as Carrie Hogg.

query.description: Full record read of Frank Ogletree / Carrie Hogg marriage entry

log.notes: Record read confirms: Frank Ogletree married Carrie Hogg, 4 December 1895, Troup County, Georgia. Index entry only — no ages or parents' names in the indexed fields. Original county register image not yet examined.

No WWI draft card found for Lewis Ogletree (b.1897, Black, Georgia). Six results: all wrong race (White), wrong state, or wrong birth date. Closest: Lewis Ogletree (b.1 March 1900, Negro, Birmingham AL) — same_person score 0.0007, definitively not a match. Lewis may not have been indexed, may have registered under a different spelling, or may have been outside Georgia during registration.

1920 census for Frank Ogletree in Georgia: 20 results. No confident match. Closest: Frank Ogletree (b.1850, Black, Meriwether County, ark:MJ6L-G6Z) — race and adjacent geography match but birth year 19 years too early; only one child (Rufus b.1903), Lewis not present. Lewis Ogletree (b.1895-1899, Black, Meriwether, ark:MJ6L-CZL) also retrieved, but same_person score 0.010 — not our subject. Frank Ogletree's 1920 household not conclusively located; he may be indexed under variant or in a different county.

9 results for Lewis Ogletree in WWI draft (coll. 1968530) near Troup County. Closest: Lewis Ogletree b.1 March 1900, Negro, Birmingham AL (same_person score 0.0007) — definitively not our subject. No Lewis Ogletree (b.March 1897, Black) registered in Georgia or Troup County found.

proof_summaries.exhaustive_search_summary: Reasonably exhaustive search declared 5 July 2026. Searched: 1910 U.S. Census (FamilySearch, collection 1727033) — positive, Frank Ogletree household with Lewis listed as son; 1900 U.S. Census (FamilySearch, collection 1325221) — negative despite eight variant queries; 1920 U.S. Census (FamilySearch, collection 1488411) — negative; Georgia County Marriages, 1785–1950 (FamilySearch, collection 1927197) — positive, Frank Ogletree and Carrie Hogg married 4 December 1895, Troup County; WWI Draft Registration (FamilySearch, collection 1968530) — negative for Lewis Ogletree (b. March 1897) despite two variant searches. No vital birth records exist for Black Georgians born in 1897 (state registration not fully in force until after 1919). The original Troup County marriage register image (ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GBZC-74X) and church records were not examined — these represent the primary avenue for advancing the conclusion to proved.
proof_summaries.narrative_markdown:
# Parents of Lewis Ogletree (Born March 1897, Georgia)

## Conclusion

The preponderance of evidence strongly suggests that Lewis Ogletree, born March 1897 in Georgia and residing in West Point, Troup County, Georgia, in 1910, was the son of **Frank Ogletree** (father, born approximately 1869 in Georgia) and **Carrie Hogg Ogletree** (mother, maiden name Hogg, died before June 1910). Frank Ogletree and Carrie Hogg married on 4 December 1895 in Troup County, Georgia — fifteen months before Lewis's birth — establishing the parental couple. Frank's widowed status in the 1910 census is consistent with Carrie having died before that enumeration.

## Source Assessment

| Source | Classification | Information Quality | Evidence Type |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1910 U.S. Census, Frank Ogletree household | Original | Indeterminate (pre-1940, respondent unknown) | Direct — relationship column |
| Georgia, County Marriages, 1785–1950 (index) | Derivative | Primary (parties stated own names) | Direct — marriage event |

The two sources are independent in institutional origin and record-creation process. The census is a federal government enumeration; the marriage record is a county clerk's civil register. No conflict exists between them.

## Evidence

### 1910 United States Census — Identifies Frank Ogletree as Father

The primary evidence for Frank Ogletree's paternity is the 1910 United States Federal Census, an original government record. Lewis Ogletree (born approximately March 1897, Black, Georgia) appears in the household of Frank Ogletree, West Point, Troup County, Georgia, with the relationship-to-head column recording him as "Son."¹ The 1910 census introduced an explicit relationship column, making this a direct statement of the parent-child relationship rather than an inference from household position. Frank Ogletree (born approximately 1869, Black, Georgia) is listed as head of household and widowed, establishing that Lewis's mother had died before the June 1910 enumeration date.

Lewis's identity in this record is confirmed by a FamilySearch identity-match score of 0.987 (the highest confidence band), consistent with his known name, birth year (~1897), birthplace (Georgia), and 1910 residence (West Point, Troup County). Because the respondent is unidentified in pre-1940 census records, the information quality for the relationship assertion is classified indeterminate rather than primary, per standard GPS evidence-classification practice.

### Georgia County Marriages, 1785–1950 — Identifies Carrie Hogg as Mother and Corroborates Paternity

A search of the FamilySearch collection "Georgia, County Marriages, 1785–1950" covering Troup County returned an entry for Frank Ogletree and Carrie Hogg, married 4 December 1895 in Troup County, Georgia.² The contracting parties stated their own names to the county marriage clerk, making the name facts primary information. The marriage is a direct event record.

This record establishes two findings: (1) Frank Ogletree is confirmed as groom, corroborating his identity as established from the 1910 census — name, county, and chronology (born ~1869, approximately 26 at marriage) are consistent; and (2) Carrie Hogg is identified as the bride and thus Frank Ogletree's wife at the time Lewis was conceived. Lewis Ogletree was born approximately fifteen months after this marriage (March 1897), establishing the couple as his likely parents.

This source is classified as a derivative — it is a FamilySearch index of the original Troup County marriage register, confirmed through two searches and a record_read of the persona (ark:/61903/1:1:KXJY-PL7). The underlying original county register image (ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GBZC-74X) was not examined.

### Corroborating FAN Evidence — Amps Hogg in the 1910 Household

The 1910 census household of Frank Ogletree also includes Amps Hogg (born approximately 1840, Black, widowed).¹ An elderly Black man sharing the Hogg surname residing in Frank's household — with no documented relationship to Frank by name — is consistent with a father-in-law relationship: Amps Hogg as Carrie Hogg's father and Lewis's maternal grandfather. His widowed status in 1910 coheres with Carrie having predeceased that date. This evidence is circumstantial but corroborates the identification of Carrie Hogg as connected to the Hogg family of West Point, Troup County.

### Negative Evidence

**1900 Census**: Despite eight variant searches of FamilySearch collection 1325221, Frank Ogletree's household was not located. Extended-family Ogletrees found in Troup County (Emanuel, b. ~1850; Adam, b. ~1847) are not the subject. Absence may reflect indexing errors, variant spellings, or temporary residence outside the county.

**1920 Census and WWI Draft**: Frank Ogletree was not conclusively located in the 1920 census (collection 1488411), and Lewis Ogletree was not found in the WWI draft registration collection (1968530) — the closest candidate (Lewis Ogletree, b. 1 March 1900, Negro, Birmingham, Alabama) scored 0.0007 on identity matching, definitively a different person. These negative results do not contradict the established parentage.

## Limitations and Path to Proved

The conclusion is rated **probable** rather than proved for three reasons: (1) the marriage source is a derivative index, not an examined original record; (2) the census relationship evidence carries indeterminate information quality due to the unknown respondent; and (3) Carrie Hogg's identification as Lewis's biological mother is an inference from the marriage and birth timing rather than a direct statement in any surviving source. No conflicting evidence was found.

To advance to proved, examination of the original Troup County marriage register image (FamilySearch ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GBZC-74X) and any church baptismal or death records for Carrie Hogg would be the highest-yield next steps.

---

¹ "United States Census, 1910," digital images, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:9FHP-PMD : accessed 5 July 2026), Frank Ogletree household, West Point, Troup County, Georgia.

² "Georgia, County Marriages, 1785-1950," digital images, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:9MVT-J7MV : accessed 5 July 2026), entry for Frank Ogletree and Carrie Hogg, 4 December 1895, Troup County, Georgia.
